result: I keep thinking about the hour just before dawn, when the sky is neither black nor blue but some bruised color that hasn’t been named because no one stays awake long enough to need it. The neighbor’s heat pump clicks off, and the absence of its hum feels like a secret I wasn’t supposed to hear. My cat sits on the windowsill, pupils dilated to lunar discs, tracking something I can’t see—maybe the ghost of a moth, maybe the day itself rehearsing its entrance. I pretend I’m not afraid of the quiet, but the truth is I’ve always trusted noise more than silence; silence has a way of rearranging the furniture inside my head while I’m still sitting in it. So I whisper the grocery list to the dark: eggs, bread, something for the guilt I keep meaning to cook down into a manageable sauce. The refrigerator answers with a mechanical sigh, the sound of civilization keeping its own hollow counsel. I remember that as a kid I believed the fridge light stayed on even when the door closed, a tiny sun for the pickles, and I still can’t shake the feeling that every closed space nurtures a private daylight I’m not invited to witness. Somewhere a sparrow strikes a match against its own throat, and the day officially begins, ordinary flammable song scattering the unnamed color from the sky. I open the door, let the cat decide in or out, and realize the list was never for groceries; it was a recipe for staying present while everything I love keeps leaving the room.

I pretend I’m not afraid of the quiet, but the truth is I’ve always trusted noise more than silence; silence has a way of rearranging the furniture inside my head while I’m still sitting in it. So I whisper the grocery list to the dark: eggs, bread, something for the guilt I keep meaning to cook down into a manageable sauce. The refrigerator answers with a mechanical sigh, the sound of civilization keeping its own hollow counsel. I remember that as a kid I believed the fridge light stayed on even when the door closed, a tiny sun for the pickles, and I still can’t shake the feeling that every closed space nurtures a private daylight I’m not invited to witness. Somewhere a sparrow strikes a match against its own throat, and the day officially begins, ordinary flammable song scattering the unnamed color from the sky. I open the door, let the cat decide in or out, and realize the list was never for groceries; it was a recipe for staying present while everything I love keeps leaving the room.
